scope.ethical_note: Public metadata and public pages are display signals, not permission to extract restricted cultural knowledge.

records.title: Yowie country: Queensland town revamps tourism brand as a prime spot for mythological creature
records.snippet: When it comes to tourism, the small town of Kilcoy may not have reefs or rainforests, but it does have the yowie.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.

records.title: Yowie
records.snippet: The Yowie is one of several names for an Australian folklore entity that is reputed to live in the Outback. The creature has origins in Aboriginal oral history.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.

records.title: Yara-ma-yha-who
records.snippet: The Yara-ma-yha-who is a legendary vampiric monster found in Southeastern Australian Aboriginal mythology. The legend is recounted by David Unaipon. According to legend, the creature resembles a little red frog-like man with a very big head, a large mouth with no teeth and suckers on the ends of its hands and feet.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.

records.title: Wandjina
records.snippet: The Wandjina, also written Wanjina and Wondjina and also known as Gulingi, are cloud and rain spirits in the beliefs of the Wanjina Wunggurr cultural bloc of Aboriginal Australians, depicted prominently in rock art in northwestern Australia. Some of the artwork in the Kimberley region of Western Australia dates back to approximately 4,000 years ago. Another closely related spirit entity is the creator being Wunngurr, a being analogous to the Rainbow Serpent in other Aboriginal peoples' belief systems, but with a different interpretation.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.

records.title: Bunyip
records.snippet: The bunyip is a creature from the aboriginal mythology of southeastern Australia, said to lurk in swamps, billabongs, creeks, riverbeds and waterholes.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.
records.location_summary: Australia: medium; Southeastern Australia (Southeastern Australia): medium

records.title: Drop bear
records.snippet: The drop bear is a hoax in contemporary Australian folklore featuring a predatory, carnivorous version of the koala. This imaginary animal is commonly spoken about in tall tales designed to scare tourists. While koalas are typically docile herbivores, drop bears are described as unusually large and vicious marsupials that inhabit treetops and attack unsuspecting people that walk beneath them by dropping onto their heads from above.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.

records.title: Nargun
records.snippet: A Nargun, according to Gunai/Kurnai tribal legends, was a fierce half-human half-stone creature that lived in the Den of Nargun, a cave under a rock overhang behind a small waterfall located in the Mitchell River National Park, Victoria, Australia. Aboriginal legend describes the Nargun as a beast entirely made of stone except for its hands, arms and breast. The fierce creature would drag unwary travellers into its den. Any weapon directed against it would be turned back on its owner.
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.

records.title: Quinkan
records.coding_notes: Initial rule-based coding; requires human review.
